---
title: "Playing is Stressful"
author: "Peter Raidl"
date: "2024-10-01"
categories: [blog, science, stress, learning, play]
draft: true
#image: "Training.png"
---

The term stress suffered from some nasty inflation in the last decade. Recently, my eight-year-old nephew said it would be stressful to visit us this month and he wants to plan ahead. First, we had a crack over it, but it also made us think about what he is taking from us adults revealing that we are stressed. Stress is generally understood as a state of feeling busy and having a rough time staying ahead of things. The psychologist J.W. Mason (1924 - 2014) described uncontrolability and **uncertainty** as being among five attributes that make a situation stressful. The others are **novelty**, **ambiguity** and **ego-involvement** SOURCE. Clearly, no single attribute of the five Mason-facors solely makes a situation stressful.

A novel sitation does not in itself create stress. But even in babies, novel situations raise attention and create excitement. Play Peekaboo with a small child and watch their reactions. You will see excitement for the first rounds, but slowly, it will lose interest. The fairly natural response of the adult is to change the theatrical play by switching the tone of voice, delaying the time till they lift their hands or changing facial expressions. Doing so keeps the child involved and excited. Especially if the variation holds some **ambiguity**. As an adult, you don't start to increase the time untill you pull away your hands systematically. Instead, you play with the delay, making it longer first and then shorter in the next game.

The last component to create a stressful situation is **ego-involvement**. A situation can be new, unpredictable and abiguous. But as long as it has no meaning to you as a person, it will not lead to a stress response. Ego-involvement corresponds to the idea of self-preservation SOURCE. All living organisms want to preserve their life and function as well as possible. But many creatures do not only try to avoid physical harm but also want to preserve their social-self SOURCE as well as the the integrity of their kin SOURCE . This might be explained in evolutionary terms. Homo sapiens is one perfect example of an animal that can not survive without a tribe or a group. Our social self, which is a function of how we think others see us (lazy, helpful, stable, egoistic) is very important for our personal survival and for that of our genome SOURCE. No wonder, social events like public speaking make us freak out and rumination and worring are often about our social encounters SOURCE.

What is the ego-involvement in the story of Peekaboo? This is hard to say for me. It might be due to the fact that a hidden person in itself is kind of unsettling and potentially dangerous to a child. It might also be a result of the Piagetian idea of object permanence. A theory that is heavily debated. Piaget described that children of a certain age cannot understand that objects or animated beings have a permanent essence . So the child, deprived of the view if its father's face, thinks that he is really gone. This is exciting for the child (as it depends on the adult).

So, the story of Peekaboo tells us a lot about stress. Firstly, there are components to a situation that make it stressful and they are in hindsite fairly straightforward (unfamiliarity, uncertainty, novelty, ambiguity and ego-involvement). Secondly, humans and many other animals (even non-mammal species, as it seems [Burghardt](https://www.cell.com/current-biology/pdf/S0960-9822(14)01333-5.pdf)) literally play with stress during their development and through their lifetime. Why?

Stress, as I describe it (you might think more of it as excitement by now) **plays a critical part in learning**. The physiological stress response is a way to (1) direct attention to important events and (2) learn from them. Psychological and physiological evidence point in the same direction here. Psychologically, for example, stress and excitement increase our **negativity bias** and **narrows down our focus of attention** SOURCE. Again, borrowing arguments from evolutioary psychology, this narrow focus on negative events makes perfect sense in the context of life-threatening events and rapidly finding already tested solutions. These solutions are described broadly as fight, flight, freeze or tend and befriend SOURCE. (or you faint if you are a human only [Barcha et al., 2005](%5BShibboleth%20Authentication%20Request%5D(https://pubmed-ncbi-nlm-nih-gov.uaccess.univie.ac.at/15944875/))). From an individual perspective, you might have realized that the easy path (if it is the wrong path [to paraphrase R. Sapolsky, talking about the PFC, 2017](https://www.amazon.de/Behave-Biology-Humans-Best-Worst/dp/1594205078) is more tempting in stressful situations. We fall back into old habits, eat calorie-dense foods, and have difficulty listening to others with empathy.

Besides attentional shifts during stress, it is also involved in learning situations. Biology ended up making us very good at learning under stress. The stress hormone cortsiol can act on basically every cell in the human body . To act on the cell's function, it has to couple to one of two distinct receptors - mineralocorticoid receptors (MR) or Corticoid receptors (CR). These receptors are basically present in every cell of the body. But they are especially dense in the PFC, amygdala, hippocampus and hypothalamus. All these brain structures are involved in learning.
